verbatim: while the Magi spend their time in the worship of the gods, in sacrifices and in prayers, implying that none but themselves have the ear of the gods.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific occupational group (Magi) that claims an exclusive religious right to communicate with the divine, effectively barring others from that specific resource.

verbatim: They prohibit personal ornament and the wearing of gold. Their dress is white, they make their bed on the ground, and their food is vegetables, cheese, and coarse bread; their staff is a reed
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the Magi as a group subject to specific ascetic regulations that restrict their access to material resources like gold and jewelry based on their status.

verbatim: Their dress is white, they make their bed on the ground, and their food is vegetables, cheese, and coarse bread; their staff is a reed and their custom is, so we are told, to stick it into the cheese and take up with it the part they eat.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The group 'Magi' is defined by occupational/religious criteria and the text explicitly lists the limited types of food they are permitted to consume, representing a restriction on their choice.

verbatim: They hold discourse of justice, and deem it impious to practise cremation; but they see no impiety in marriage with a mother or daughter, as Sotion relates in his twenty-third book.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific restriction on the Magi regarding the disposal of the dead, framing cremation as an impious act they are barred from performing.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be recognized as inventors
verbatim: They also laid down laws on the subject of justice, which they ascribed to Hermes; and they deified those animals which are serviceable to man. They also claimed to have invented geometry, astronomy, and arithmetic.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text attributes the creation/monopoly of specific intellectual resources (geometry, astronomy, arithmetic) to the Egyptian people as a collective identity group.

extracted_rules.rule: Noble access to the Sage title
extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the title of Sage
verbatim: Herodotus, Duris, and Democritus are agreed that Thales was the son of Examyas and Cleobulina, and belonged to the Thelidae who are Phoenicians, and among the noblest of the descendants of Cadmus and Agenor. As Plato testifies, he was one of the Seven Sages. He was the first to receive the name of Sage, in the archonship of Damasias at Athens, when the term was applied to all the Seven Sages, as Demetrius of Phalerum mentions in his List of Archons.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text links Thales' inclusion among the Seven Sages and his receipt of the title 'Sage' to his 'noble' lineage. In the context of early Greek society described by Diogenes, high birth (Nobility) was a prerequisite for such public recognition and immaterial honors.

extracted_rules.rule: Citizen right to give political advice
extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in state affairs
verbatim: He was admitted to citizenship at Miletus when he came to that town along with Nileos, who had been expelled from Phoenicia. . . After engaging in politics he became a student of nature. . . Thales is also credited with having given excellent advice on political matters. For instance, when Croesus sent to Miletus offering terms of alliance, he frustrated the plan; and this proved the salvation of the city when Cyrus obtained the victory.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Thales' engagement in politics and his ability to influence the city's alliance decisions are explicitly preceded by his admission to citizenship. This demonstrates that political participation and formal influence over state affairs were resources reserved for those with the status of citizen.

verbatim: Hieronymus of Rhodes in the second book of his Scattered Notes relates that, in order to show how easy it is to grow rich, Thales, foreseeing that it would be a good season for olives, rented all the oil-mills and thus amassed a fortune. Because, having created a monopoly, he could charge what he pleased.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the ability to corner a market by renting ('leasing') production means as a function of having the initial capital to do so. This identifies the 'Wealthy' as the group having access to the resource of large-scale commercial contracts and monopolies.

verbatim: Hermippus in his Lives refers to Thales the story which is told by some of Socrates, namely, that he used to say there were three blessings for which he was grateful to Fortune: first, that I was born a human being and not one of the brutes; next, that I was born a man and not a woman; thirdly, a Greek and not a barbarian.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly lists being born a man rather than a woman as one of the three primary blessings or advantages of existence in Greek society. This reflects a normative rule where gender determines the starting level of social 'blessing' or resource access.

verbatim: Hermippus in his Lives refers to Thales the story which is told by some of Socrates, namely, that he used to say there were three blessings for which he was grateful to Fortune: first, that I was born a human being and not one of the brutes; next, that I was born a man and not a woman; thirdly, a Greek and not a barbarian.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies being born a Greek as a fundamental 'blessing' compared to being born a 'barbarian' (non-Greek). This establishes a clear hierarchy based on ethnicity and origin regarding the perceived quality and rights of an individual.

verbatim: In process of time certain people of Lebedus, having purchased a catch of fish thereabouts, obtained possession of the tripod, and, quarrelling with the fishermen about it, put in to Cos, and, when they could not settle the dispute, reported the fact to Miletus, their mother-city. The Milesians, when their embassies were disregarded, made war upon Cos
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule or established norm where colonial settlements (Lebedus) refer unresolved legal or diplomatic disputes to their 'mother-city' (Miletus) for resolution or intervention, indicating a lack of total judicial autonomy for the sub-group.

verbatim: Solon, the son of Execestides, was born at Salamis. His first achievement was the σεισάχθεια or Law of Release, which he introduced at Athens; its effect was to ransom persons and property. For men used to borrow money on personal security, and many were forced from poverty to become serfs or daylabourers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes Solon's historical Law of Release (Seisachtheia) which specifically prevented citizens from being forced into serfdom due to debt and restored their property.

verbatim: Megara and Athens laid rival claims to his birthplace Salamis, and after many defeats the Athenians passed a decree punishing with death any man who should propose a renewal of the Salaminian war.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes a specific decree in Athens that criminalized the political act of proposing a new war, removing the right of citizens to speak on this specific policy under threat of death.

verbatim: Every citizen pays a tithe of his property, not to me but to a fund for defraying the cost of the public sacrifices or any other charges on the State or the expenditure on any war which may come upon us.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pisistratus's letter outlines a rule where citizens must pay 10% of their property to the state, reducing their total material resources to fund public needs.

verbatim: He seems to have enacted some admirable laws; for instance, if any man neglects to provide for his parents, he shall be disfranchised; moreover there is a similar penalty for the spendthrift who runs through his patrimony.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Solon's laws tied the retention of citizenship rights to specific domestic obligations toward parents and estate management.

verbatim: Again, not to have a settled occupation is made a crime for which any one may, if he pleases, impeach the offender.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law made a lack of specific occupation a criminal offense, effectively mandating economic activity for members of the group.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to speak in the Assembly
verbatim: Lysias, however, in his speech against Nicias ascribes this law to Draco, and to Solon another depriving open profligates of the right to speak in the Assembly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Specific moral conduct (defined as profligacy) led to the removal of the immaterial resource of political participation in the Assembly.

verbatim: Excellent, too, is his provision that the guardian of an orphan should not marry the mother of his ward, and that the next heir who would succeed on the death of the orphans should be disqualified from acting as their guardian.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Solonian law restricted the marital choices of guardians and the eligibility for guardianship based on inheritance interests to protect the orphan's resources.

verbatim: Furthermore, that no engraver of seals should be allowed to retain an impression of the ring which he has sold
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is a specific professional regulation for engravers that limits what they can keep from their labor.

verbatim: That the magistrate found intoxicated should be punished with death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: A law specifically targeting those in holding a certain occupational status (magistrates) with an extreme penalty for a specific behavior.

verbatim: That is, I resume the privileges which the Athenians swore to confer upon Codrus and his family, although later they took them away... I arrogate to myself no undue share of reputation and honour, but merely such stated privileges as belonged to the kings in former times.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies specific political and honorific resources linked to the lineage of Codrus.

verbatim: Old sire, two offers of marriage are made to me; the one bride is in wealth and birth my equal; The other is my superior... See there, yonder boys will tell you the whole tale... they were saying, Keep to your own sphere.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text presents a social norm (expressed through a sage's advice) that individuals should marry within their own social rank/sphere rather than marry up.

verbatim: And this is what you must do if you want to strengthen your absolute rule: put to death those among the citizens who are pre-eminent, whether they are hostile to you or not. For to an absolute ruler even a friend is an object of suspicion.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is presented as a political maxim or rule of thumb for tyrants in the Greek world, specifically attributed to Thrasybulus's advice to Periander, suggesting that under absolute rule, distinguished status (pre-eminence) among citizens stripped them of their right to life.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be entertained as guest
verbatim: The servant delivered his message and was ordered by Solon to tell him that men as a rule choose their guests from among their own countrymen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Solon explicitly states a general rule of Greek social practice (xenia) where the status of being a fellow countryman/citizen is a prerequisite for being chosen as a guest, thereby excluding foreigners like Anacharsis by default.

verbatim: At any rate he was seen in Lacedaemon laughing to himself in a lonely spot; and when some one suddenly appeared and asked him why he laughed when no one was near, he replied, That is just the reason. And Aristoxenus says that the reason why he remained obscure was that he belonged to no city but to a village and that an unimportant one.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that Myson's obscurity was caused by his lack of citizenship in a 'city' (polis), belonging only to a 'village'. In the Greek context, legal standing and fame were tied to being a member (citizen) of a recognized city-state.

verbatim: For if Pisistratus had attacked the Athenians while they were still serfs and before they had good laws, he would have secured power in perpetuity by the enslavement of the citizens. But, as it is, he is reducing to subjection men who are no cowards... for it is hard to contrive that men brought up as free men under the best laws should be slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text contrasts a past state where Athenians were serfs with a current state where 'good laws' and 'free' status protect citizens from being legally or practically treated as slaves, even under a tyrant.

verbatim: He was eminent for wealth and noble birth... He began to study philosophy at Athens in the archonship of Callias i.e. 456 b.c. when he was twenty; Demetrius of Phalerum states this in his list of archons; and at Athens they say he remained for thirty years.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text links the period of Anaxagoras's study to the 'archonship of Callias'. In the context of 5th-century Athens described, the office of Archon was reserved for those of specific wealth and lineage (Pentakosiomedimnoi/Hippeis), implying that the criteria of wealth and nobility granted access to high political office.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from legal prosecution for beliefs
verbatim: Sotion in his Succession of the Philosophers says that he was indicted by Cleon on a charge of impiety, because he declared the sun to be a mass of red-hot metal; that his pupil Pericles defended him, and he was fined five talents and banished.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal rule where expressing certain physical theories about the cosmos (the sun as a red-hot metal) was categorized as 'impiety' (asebeia), leading to criminal indictment, fines, and banishment.

verbatim: He was eminent for wealth and noble birth, and furthermore for magnanimity, in that he gave up his patrimony to his relations. For, when they accused him of neglecting it, he replied, Why then do you not look after it?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage shows that 'relations' (kin) had a recognized claim or interest in the 'patrimony' (family estate). Anaxagoras's act of giving it up to them demonstrates that lineage determined who had the right to possess and manage ancestral property.

verbatim: At length he retired to Lampsacus and there died. And when the magistrates of the city asked if there was anything he would like done for him, he replied that he would like them to grant an annual holiday to the boys in the month in which he died; and the custom is kept up to this day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes that the city of Lampsacus established a rule (custom) based on Anaxagoras's request, granting a specific group (schoolboys) a recurring resource (a holiday). The author confirms this rule is still 'kept up to this day'.

verbatim: He was formidable in public speaking, according to Idomeneus; moreover, as Xenophon tells us, the Thirty forbade him to teach the art of words.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a specific legal prohibition issued by the governing body (The Thirty) against Socrates in his capacity as a teacher/philosopher, restricting his professional activity by forbidding the teaching of 'the art of words'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to take a second wife
verbatim: For they say that the Athenians were short of men and, wishing to increase the population, passed a decree permitting a citizen to marry one Athenian woman and have children by another; and that Socrates accordingly did so.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal decree passed in Athens during a period of population decline that expanded the marital rights of male citizens, allowing them to have children by a second woman alongside a primary wife.

verbatim: At all events, when some one told him that Antisthenes’ mother was a Thracian, he replied, Nay, did you expect a man so noble to have been born of two Athenian parents?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text refers to the common social and legal understanding in Athens that individuals of foreign extraction (like Thracians) were by definition excluded from having two Athenian parents, a status typically required for full civic nobility or certain rights.

verbatim: He made Crito ransom Phaedo who, having been taken prisoner in the war, was kept in degrading slavery, and so won him for philosophy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the legal and social reality where prisoners of war were converted into the resource-less status of slaves, requiring a ransom to regain their freedom.

verbatim: Socrates is guilty of refusing to recognize the gods recognized by the state, and of introducing other new divinities. He is also guilty of corrupting the youth. The penalty demanded is death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The official indictment recorded in the text shows a legal rule in Athens prohibiting citizens from substituting state-recognized gods with new ones upon pain of death.

verbatim: When this caused an uproar among the judges, he said, Considering my services, I assess the penalty at maintenance in the Prytaneum at the public expense.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text refers to the existing institution of the Prytaneum where the city provided free meals to citizens deemed to have provided exceptional service (wealth/merit criteria).

verbatim: Again, when Charmides offered him some slaves in order that he might derive an income from them, he declined the offer
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights that slaves were considered productive assets whose labor/income belonged entirely to the owner, not the slave.

verbatim: About this time he was banished by the Athenians for siding with Sparta. When he was in Ephesus and had a sum of money, he entrusted one half of it to Megabyzus... to keep until his return
extracted_rules.reasoning: Xenophon's case illustrates the legal rule of 'ostracism' or banishment where citizens lost their right to live in their home city due to political affiliations.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from legal prosecution for neglect
verbatim: and, in a speech written for the freedman whom Xenophon prosecuted for neglect of duty, Dinarchus mentions that his two sons... also went with him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mentions a specific legal action (prosecution for neglect of duty) available against freedmen, implying they remained legally bound to certain obligations to their former masters.

verbatim: and that wealth too is productive of pleasure, though not desirable for its own sake. They affirm that mental affections can be known, but not the objects from which they come; and they abandoned the study of nature because of its apparent uncertainty, but fastened on logical inquiries because of their utility.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Cyrenaic philosophers (followers of Aristippus) defined wealth as a material instrument that increases the amount of pleasure an individual can obtain, establishing a link between economic resources and human development (defined here as pleasure).

verbatim: that the sage will not give way to envy or love or superstition, since these weaknesses are due to mere empty opinion; he will, however, feel pain and fear, these being natural affections; and that wealth too is productive of pleasure, though not desirable for its own sake.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines 'the sage' (the philosopher/educated man) as a specific group that, by virtue of their intellectual progress, gains a specific immaterial resource: the capacity to be unaffected by envy and superstition.

verbatim: Phaedo was a native of Elis, of noble family, who on the fall of that city was taken captive and forcibly consigned to a house of ill-fame. But he would close the door and so contrive to join Socrates’ circle, and in the end Socrates induced Alcibiades or Crito with their friends to ransom him; from that time onwards he studied philosophy as became a free man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly links the status of a 'free man' (liberated from slavery) with the right or propriety of engaging in philosophical study, implying that as a slave he was barred from the formal pursuit of this immaterial resource.

verbatim: When Pericles promised to support him and urged him to come to him, his reply was, I will not part with my free speech for money. There was another Simon, who wrote treatises On Rhetoric; another, a physician, in the time of Seleucus Nicanor; and a third who was a sculptor.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Simon the cobbler distinguishes his status as a free-speaking individual from one whose speech is owned or influenced by a patron. This reflects a norm where 'free speech' (parrhesia) is a resource held by the autonomous citizen.

verbatim: Tell me, Euryclides, who they are who violate the mysteries? Euryclides replied, Those who disclose them to the uninitiated. Then you violate them, said Theodorus, when you explain them to the uninitiated. Yet he would hardly have escaped from being brought before the Areopagus if Demetrius of Phalerum had not rescued him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a formal boundary between the initiated and the uninitiated; the latter are legally barred from receiving information about the mysteries. Violating this rule (disclosure) was a criminal offense punishable by the Areopagus.

verbatim: That Plato acted as choregus at Athens, the cost being defrayed by Dion, is stated by Athenodorus in the eighth book of a work entitled Walks.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an instance of a wealthy individual (Dion) defraying the costs for a choregus (Plato). This reflects the Athenian institution of liturgies, where the wealthiest citizens and residents were legally required to fund public services like theatrical performances.

verbatim: And Aristoxenus asserts that he went on service three times, first to Tanagra, secondly to Corinth, and thirdly at Delium, where also he obtained the prize of valour.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of Plato, a citizen of Athens, serving in multiple military campaigns (Tanagra, Corinth, Delium) illustrates the general rule in Greek city-states that citizens owed compulsory military service to the state.

verbatim: He was born, according to some, in Aegina, in the house of Phidiades, the son of Thales, as Favorinus states in his Miscellaneous History, for his father had been sent along with others to Aegina to settle in the island, but returned to Athens when the Athenians were expelled by the Lacedaemonians
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato's father was sent to Aegina as part of a group of settlers. This refers to the practice of cleruchies, where Athenian citizens were granted land and the right to settle in conquered or allied territories.

verbatim: And then Charmandrus, the son of Charmandrides, indicted him on a capital charge according to the law in force among the Aeginetans, to the effect that the first Athenian who set foot upon the island should be put to death without a trial.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal statute enacted by the Aeginetans targeting Athenians specifically (based on their citizenship/origin) which stripped them of their right to life and trial upon landing on the island.

verbatim: The assembly decided not to put him to death but to sell him just as if he were a prisoner of war.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates a standard practice or rule where individuals categorized as prisoners of war (or treated as such by the assembly) could be legally sold into slavery, thereby losing their personal freedom.

verbatim: To drink to excess was nowhere becoming, he used to say, save at the feasts of the god who was the giver of wine.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato's statement reflects a social norm/convention where excessive drinking, generally socially prohibited or discouraged, was explicitly permitted or made acceptable during specific religious festivals dedicated to Dionysus.

verbatim: One day, when Xenocrates had come in, Plato asked him to chastise his slave, since he was unable to do it himself because he was in a passion. Further, it is alleged that he said to one of his slaves, I would have given you a flogging, had I not been in a passion.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The interactions described confirm a general rule in Greek society that slaves were subject to corporal punishment (flogging/chastisement) at the discretion of their masters, indicating a lack of protection from such treatment.

verbatim: the estate in Iphistiadae, bounded on the north by the road from the temple at Cephisia... this it shall be unlawful for anyone to sell or alienate, but it shall be the property of the boy Adeimantus to all intents and purposes
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato's will establishes a legal restriction on a specific property, preventing the future owner (Adeimantus) from selling or transferring it, which represents a legal constraint on property rights common in testamentary rules.

verbatim: I enfranchise Artemis. I leave four household servants, Tychon, Bictas, Apollonides and Dionysius.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The will explicitly grants freedom to a slave (Artemis), demonstrating the existence of the legal mechanism of manumission where a master can grant freedom to a slave through a testamentary rule.

verbatim: The natural rulers are the males, not only among men, but also among the other animals; for the males everywhere exert wide-reaching rule over the females.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly defines a 'rule according to nature' where males are established as the specific group possessing authority over females across both human and animal contexts.

verbatim: Hereditary rule is exemplified by that of the Lacedaemonian kings, for the office of king is confined to a certain family. And the same system is in force for the kingdom of Macedonia; for there too the office of king goes by birth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal/political arrangement in Sparta (Lacedaemon) and Macedonia where access to the highest political office (the kingship) is restricted to specific lineages/noble families.

verbatim: Oligarchy is that form in which there is a property-qualification for the holding of office; for the rich are fewer than the poor.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In describing the oligarchic form of government, the author states that the resource of eligibility for office is tied to wealth, granting it to the rich while implicitly denying it to those without the required property.

verbatim: At Carthage the kingship is regulated by law, the office being put up for sale.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes a specific local law in Carthage where the highest political resource (the kingship) is acquired through purchase, thereby restricting access to those with significant material resources.

verbatim: Written law is that under which we live in different cities, but that which has arisen out of custom is called unwritten law; for instance, not to appear in the market-place undressed or in women’s attire. There is no statute forbidding this, but nevertheless we abstain from such conduct because it is prohibited by an unwritten law.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies an unwritten social rule (norm) that restricts men's choice of clothing in public spaces based on gender boundary expectations.

verbatim: And, whereas Plato exempted from fees all who came to him, you levy tribute on them and collect it whether they will or no.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a shift in the Academy's rules under Speusippus where student attendance was no longer free, but subject to involuntary fees or 'tribute', unlike the previous practice under Plato.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from the oath in court
verbatim: His words were entirely worthy of credit, so much so that, although it was illegal for witnesses to give evidence unsworn, the Athenians allowed Xenocrates alone to do so.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal exemption granted by the Athenian state to Xenocrates, allowing him to bypass the general rule that all legal witnesses must be under oath.

verbatim: Such was his character, and yet, when he was unable to pay the tax levied on resident aliens, the Athenians put him up for sale. And Demetrius of Phalerum purchased him, thereby making twofold restitution, to Xenocrates of his liberty, and to the Athenians of their tax.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage highlights a rule in Athens where resident aliens (metoiokoi) who failed to pay their specific tax could be sold into slavery by the state.

verbatim: Afterwards my father, who had cheated the revenue in some way, was sold with all his family. And I, then a not ungraceful youngster, was bought by a certain rhetorician, who on his death left me all he had.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a legal consequence where the family of a freedman/slave could be sold by the state (likely in Olbia/Borysthenes) due to the father's financial crimes against the revenue.

verbatim: And I, then a not ungraceful youngster, was bought by a certain rhetorician, who on his death left me all he had. And I burnt his books, scraped everything together, came to Athens and turned philosopher.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Bion's biography confirms that a master could legally name a slave as his heir, which effectively granted the slave freedom and property upon the master's death.

verbatim: To return to Aristotle: he came to Athens, was head of his school for thirteen years, and then withdrew to Chalcis because he was indicted for impiety by Eurymedon the hierophant, or, according to Favorinus in his Miscellaneous History, by Demophilus, the ground of the charge being the hymn he composed to the aforesaid Hermias, as well as the following inscription for his statue at Delphi
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal capacity held by the Hierophant (a religious official or occupation) to bring charges of impiety against individuals in Athens, a rule-governed procedure in their society.

verbatim: Aristippus in his first book On the Luxury of the Ancients says that Aristotle fell in love with a concubine of Hermias, and married her with his consent, and in an excess of delight sacrificed to a weak woman as the Athenians did to Demeter of Eleusis
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that Aristotle required the consent of Hermias to marry the woman (who was in a dependent/concubine status under Hermias's ownership/control), indicating that those in such status lacked the independent right to contract marriage.

verbatim: Eurymedon, the priest of Deo’s mysteries, was once about to indict Aristotle for impiety, but he, by a draught of poison, escaped prosecution. This then was an easy way of vanquishing unjust calumnies.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal mechanism (indictment for impiety) handled by religious officials (the priest of Deo) that targeted individuals based on their teachings or beliefs, specifically identifying a philosopher as the target.

verbatim: And when the girl shall be grown up she shall be given in marriage to Nicanor; but if anything happen to the girl (which heaven forbid and no such thing will happen) before her marriage, or when she is married but before there are children, Nicanor shall have full powers, both with regard to the child and with regard to everything else, to administer in a manner worthy both of himself and of us.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle's will demonstrates the legal norm where a father or his designated male executor (kyrios) determines the marriage partner for a daughter without her consent being mentioned as a requirement.

verbatim: The executors and Nicanor, in memory of me and of the steady affection which Herpyllis has borne towards me, shall take care of her in every other respect and, if she desires to be married, shall see that she be given to one not unworthy; and besides what she has already received they shall give her a talent of silver out of the estate and three handmaids whomsoever she shall choose besides the maid she has at present and the man-servant Pyrrhaeus;
extracted_rules.reasoning: The will provides a testamentary rule ensuring that a long-term partner/concubine (Herpyllis) receives significant material resources (money and slaves) and assistance in future marriage, showing a form of protected financial status.

verbatim: Ambracis shall be given her freedom, and on my daughter’s marriage shall receive 500 drachmas and the maid whom she now has. And to Thale shall be given, in addition to the maid whom she has and who was bought, a thousand drachmas and a maid. And Simon, in addition to the money before paid to him towards another servant, shall either have a servant purchased for him or receive a further sum of money. And Tycho, Philo, Olympius and his child shall have their freedom when my daughter is married.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text lists specific rules for the manumission of slaves upon the death of the master or the occurrence of specific family milestones (like the marriage of a child).

extracted_rules.rule: Protection from sale for household slaves
verbatim: None of the servants who waited upon me shall be sold but they shall continue to be employed; and when they arrive at the proper age they shall have their freedom if they deserve it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle's will explicitly forbid the sale of his personal domestic servants, establishing a rule of continued employment and eventual manumission rather than disposal as liquid assets.

verbatim: Although his reputation stood so high, nevertheless for a short time he had to leave the country with all the other philosophers, when Sophocles the son of Amphiclides proposed a law that no philosopher should preside over a school except by permission of the Senate and the people, under penalty of death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific Athenian law that restricted philosophers from leading educational institutions without state authorization. Failure to comply was a capital offense, indicating a significant restriction on the group's professional autonomy.

verbatim: The next year, however, the philosophers returned, as Philo had prosecuted Sophocles for making an illegal proposal. Whereupon the Athenians repealed the law, fined Sophocles five talents, and voted the recall of the philosophers, in order that Theophrastus also might return and live there as before.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The repeal of Sophocles' law and the subsequent 'recall' of the philosophers confirms that the preceding rule effectively forced the entire group into exile, as they could no longer practice their occupation.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a public funeral procession
verbatim: And according to the story all the Athenians, out of respect for the man, escorted his bier on foot.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an act of collective honoring by the Athenian citizenry (all Athenians) towards a specific class of person (the head of the Peripatetic school). While framed as respect for a specific individual (Theophrastus), in the context of Diogenes Laertius' biographies, it illustrates the normative practice of granting high-status intellectual leaders public funerary honors normally reserved for statesmen.

verbatim: His eloquence and sonorousness of diction appear from the following fact; he speaks of a penniless maiden as follows: A grievous burden to a father is a girl, when for lack of a dowry she runs past the flower of her age.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social reality where the absence of a dowry (wealth) prevents a woman from marrying during her reproductive prime, treating marriage as conditional on property transfer.

verbatim: Pompylus and Threpta have long been emancipated and have done me much service... And of my slaves I at once emancipate Molon and Timon and Parmeno; to Manes and Callias I give their freedom on condition that they stay four years in the garden and work there together and that their conduct is free from blame.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The will of Theophrastus lists specific legal acts of manumission, showing that slaves can obtain the resource of freedom through the owner's legal declarations, sometimes with conditions of continued service.

verbatim: I give and bequeath to them the maidservant Somatale. ... I also devise Carion to Demotimus, and Donax to Neleus. But Euboeus must be sold.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly categorizes certain individuals (slaves) as property to be bequeathed, gifted, or sold by the owner, thereby confirming their exclusion from the right to own themselves or other property.

verbatim: on condition that no one alienates the property or devotes it to his private use, but so that they hold it like a temple in joint possession... Let the community consist of Hipparchus, Neleus, Strato, Callinus, Demotimus, Demaratus, Callisthenes, Melantes, Pancreon, Nicippus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The will specifies that the garden property must be held in joint trust for a specific group of friends to use for a school, contrasting with standard individual private ownership and emphasizing the restrictions on land transfer in the Peripatetic context.

verbatim: Agathon should be set free after two years, and the litter-bearers Ophelio and Posidonius after four years’ further service.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where freedom is granted to specific slaves as a future resource, contingent upon a period of continued labor for the estate's heirs.

verbatim: Antisthenes, wrote the son of Antisthenes, was an Athenian. It was said, however, that he was not of pure Attic blood. Hence his reply to one who taunted him with this: The mother of the gods too is a Phrygian. For his mother was supposed to have been a Thracian. Hence it was that, when he had distinguished himself in the battle of Tanagra, he gave Socrates occasion to remark that, if both his parents had been Athenians, he would not have turned out so brave.
Being reproached because his parents were not both free-born, Nor were they both wrestlers, quoth he, but yet I am a wrestler.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights that Antisthenes faced social and legal taunts because he was not of 'pure Attic blood' (one parent being a Thracian) and because his parents were not both 'free-born'. This reflects the Athenian legal and social norm where such lineage precluded one from being considered a 'proper' or full citizen, impacting their standing in society.

verbatim: He used to recommend the Athenians to vote that asses are horses. When they deemed this absurd, his reply was, But yet generals are found among you who had had no training, but were merely elected.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Antisthenes mocks the Athenian system by pointing out that men are elected as generals without being required to have any actual military training, treating the position as a matter of democratic vote rather than professional competence.

verbatim: Virtue is the same for women as for men. Good actions are fair and evil actions foul.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is a philosophical principle taught by Antisthenes. While likely a contrast to prevailing social norms, it establishes a rule within the Cynic school that the capacity for and definition of virtue is not restricted by gender.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the city
verbatim: Diogenes was a native of Sinope, son of Hicesius, a banker. Diocles relates that he went into exile because his father was entrusted with the money of the state and adulterated the coinage.

When the god gave him permission to alter the political currency, not understanding what this meant, he adulterated the state coinage, and when he was detected, according to some he was banished, while according to others he voluntarily quitted the city for fear of consequences.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal consequence (banishment/exile) for the act of debasing or adulterating state currency. While Diogenes is an individual, the context regarding his father being 'entrusted with the money of the state' implies a professional/official rule for those managing state finances (bankers).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the city
verbatim: One version is that his father entrusted him with the money and that he debased it, in consequence of which the father was imprisoned and died, while the son fled, came to Delphi.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage indicates that the penalty for debasing currency included imprisonment (for the father) and forced flight/exile, reflecting a societal rule regarding the protection of the state's monetary integrity through the removal or detention of offenders.

verbatim: To Xeniades who purchased him he said, You must obey me, although I am a slave; for, if a physician or a steersman were in slavery, he would be obeyed. Eubulus in his book entitled The Sale of Diogenes tells us that this was how he trained the sons of Xeniades. After their other studies he taught them to ride, to shoot with the bow, to sling stones and to hurl javelins.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the case of educated or 'expert' slaves (pedagogues, physicians), the social and legal convention allowed them to exercise authority and commands over free children or owners in the specific context of their expertise (training, medicine), granting them a degree of influential power despite their slave status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to specific diet and appearance
verbatim: In the house too he taught them to wait upon themselves, and to be content with plain fare and water to drink. He used to make them crop their hair close and to wear it unadorned, and to go lightly clad, barefoot, silent, and not looking about them in the streets.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the specific disciplines imposed on the sons of Xeniades by their slave-tutor Diogenes. Although the children are technically free, the 'rule' of their upbringing under Diogenes' system stripped them of luxuries (finery, long hair, choice of food), establishing a norm of asceticism.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of choice in meal timing
verbatim: To one who asked what was the proper time for lunch, he said, If a rich man, when you will; if a poor man, when you can.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social reality where financial status dictates the ability to choose one's own meal times, with the poor being constrained by necessity rather than will.

verbatim: The Athenians urged him to become initiated, and told him that in the other world those who have been initiated enjoy a special privilege. It would be ludicrous, quoth he, if Agesilaus and Epaminondas are to dwell in the mire, while certain folk of no account will live in the Isles of the Blest because they have been initiated.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage refers to the Athenian belief/convention that religious initiation (likely the Eleusinian Mysteries) grants material-immaterial benefits (Isles of the Blest) in the afterlife compared to non-initiates.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal punishment during trial
verbatim: One day after listening to a couple of lawyers disputing, he condemned them both, saying that the one had no doubt stolen, but the other had not lost anything.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of lawyers and disputes reflects the legal context of the time where slave status significantly altered legal rights, though this specific verbatim is weaker than others in the text, the surrounding context of the 'hound' and the 'sale' of Diogenes clarifies the slave/free distinction.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exclusive marriage and paternity
verbatim: He advocated community of wives, recognizing no other marriage than a union of the man who persuades with the woman who consents. And for this reason he thought sons too should be held in common.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While this is a philosophical advocacy, Diogenes presents it as a rule for the 'only true commonwealth,' contrasting it with the standard Athenian practice of exclusive domestic rights.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exercise authority over free children
verbatim: Thus Xeniades came to buy him, and took him to Corinth and set him over his own children and entrusted his whole household to him. And he administered it in all respects in such a manner that Xeniades used to go about saying, A good genius has entered my house.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of Greek pedagogy, a slave (paidagogos) could be granted the resource of authority over the master's children and domestic affairs.

verbatim: Monimus of Syracuse was a pupil of Diogenes; and, according to Sosicrates, he was in the service of a certain Corinthian banker... until at length his master dismissed him; and he then straightway devoted himself to Diogenes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a master (a banker) dismissing his servant (Monimus) upon the master's decision, illustrating the master's power to unilaterally terminate the relationship without the subordinate's consent or specific legal protections.

verbatim: So he turned his property into money,—for he belonged to a distinguished family,—and having thus collected about 200 talents, distributed that sum among his fellow-citizens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Crates, as a wealthy individual, chooses a specific group—his fellow-citizens—as the recipients of his material wealth, demonstrating that citizenship was the criterion for eligibility in such private redistribution of assets.

verbatim: Eratosthenes tells us that by Hipparchia, of whom we shall presently speak, he had a son born to him named Pasicles, and after he had ceased to be a cadet on service, Crates took him to a brothel.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of a son 'ceasing to be a cadet' implies a mandatory period of service (ephebeia) tied to a specific age group (young men), contrasting with the lack of such obligation for children or older adults.

verbatim: The girl chose and, adopting the same dress, went about with her husband and lived with him in public and went out to dinners with him. Accordingly she appeared at the banquet given by Lysimachus... Hipparchia showed no sign of alarm or of the perturbation natural in a woman.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hipparchia's presence at public banquets and her public life are framed as exceptional behavior 'natural' for her philosophy but contrary to the standard expectations for women, implying a general rule of exclusion or private seclusion for women.

verbatim: Menippus, also a Cynic, was by descent a Phoenician—a slave, as Achaïcus in his treatise on Ethics says. Diocles further informs us that his master was a citizen of Pontus and was named Baton. But as avarice made him very resolute in begging, he succeeded in becoming a Theban.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific historical figure (Menippus) who was a slave but managed to change his status and location (becoming a Theban citizen/resident) by accumulating money (through begging 'avarice'). This implies a social rule where slaves could possess some funds to change their legal or social standing.

verbatim: Hermippus says that he lent out money by the day and got a nickname from doing so. For he used to make loans on bottomry and take security, thus accumulating a large fortune. At last, however, he fell a victim to a plot, was robbed of all, and in despair ended his days by hanging himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Menippus, as a foreigner (Phoenician/Theban by adoption) in various Greek contexts, is described as accumulating wealth through bottomry (maritime loans) and money-lending rather than land. In the Greek context described, non-citizens (metics) were generally barred from owning real property, forcing them into liquid capital markets like lending.

extracted_rules.rule: Citizen keys of the city trust
verbatim: The people of Athens held Zeno in high honour, as is proved by their depositing with him the keys of the city walls, and their honouring him with a golden crown and a bronze statue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific civic practice in Athens where a highly honored resident, based on their reputation and contribution to the city's youth, is granted symbolic and practical control over the city's defenses (the keys).

verbatim: it has seemed good to the people— and may it turn out well—to bestow praise upon Zeno of Citium... and to build him a tomb in the Ceramicus at the public cost.
And that for the making of the crown and the building of the tomb, the people shall now elect five commissioners from all Athenians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The decree establishes the rule that the Athenian state provides public funds and land in the prestigious Ceramicus cemetery for the burial of those officially recognized for their virtue and service to the city.

verbatim: We are told that he was once chastising a slave for stealing, and when the latter pleaded that it was his fate to steal, Yes, and to be beaten too, said Zeno.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage illustrates the accepted social and legal norm that a master has the right to physically beat a slave as a punishment for a crime like theft.

verbatim: Again, the Stoics say that the wise man will take part in politics, if nothing hinders him—so, for instance, Chrysippus in the first book of his work On Various Types of Life—since thus he will restrain vice and promote virtue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a normative rule for the 'Wise' group within Stoic philosophy, asserting their intended role in governance and political life.

verbatim: Again, it is objected, in the Republic he lays down community of wives, and at line 200 prohibits the building of temples, lawcourts and gymnasia in cities
It is also their doctrine that amongst the wise there should be a community of wives with free choice of partners, as Zeno says in his Republic
extracted_rules.reasoning: Zeno's 'Republic', while a theoretical work, is treated in this text as a set of rules for an ideal city-state where women do not belong to one husband but are shared among the community of the wise.

verbatim: They declare that he alone is free and bad men are slaves, freedom being power of independent action, whereas slavery is privation of the same
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Stoic rule defines 'freedom' as a resource (the power of independent action) as belonging exclusively to the wise, while categorizing all non-virtuous people as being in a state of slavery.

verbatim: Cleanthes indeed holds that all souls continue to exist until the general conflagration; but Chrysippus says that only the souls of the wise do so.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Chrysippus distinguishes a specific subgroup (the wise) from the general population based on their intellectual/philosophical state, granting them the unique resource of their souls surviving until the cosmic conflagration, whereas others do not.

verbatim: That of the female is according to them sterile, being, as Sphaerus says, without tension, scanty, and watery.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a biological rule within Stoic thought where women are denied the 'resource' of fertile/generative semen compared to the male equivalent, based on the criterion of gender.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain one's own earnings
verbatim: For Zeno used to discipline him to this and bid him return him an obol from his wages. A slave allowed by his master to hire himself out to another master was bound by Attic law to refund to his own master a part (ἀποφορά) of the wages he received. Zeno claimed a part of his pupil’s earnings.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly references are a specific Attic legal requirement (apophora) where individuals (specifically slaves or those in a similar subordinate relationship like Cleanthes to Zeno in this pedagogical context) were required to pay back a portion of their external earnings to their 'master' or supervisor.

extracted_rules.rule: Investigation of livelihood for the able-bodied
extracted_rules.resource: Protection from legal inquiry into income
verbatim: He is said to have been brought into court to answer the inquiry how so sturdy a fellow as he made his living, and then to have been acquitted on producing as his witnesses the gardener in whose garden he drew water and the woman who sold the meal which he used to crush. The Areopagites were satisfied and voted him a donation of ten minas, which Zeno forbade him to accept.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This describes a legal proceeding in the Areopagus where a man visibly healthy/sturdy but appearing poor was legally compelled to prove he had a legitimate source of income, indicating that the poor lacked the resource of privacy regarding their means of survival.

verbatim: Hecato says that he came to the study of philosophy, because the property which he had inherited from his father had been confiscated to the king’s treasury.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal/political mechanism where an individual's inherited wealth was seized by the state (the king's treasury), indicating that property rights for wealthy subjects were subject to royal confiscation.

verbatim: And another besides, a son of the former, court-physician to Ptolemy, who on a false charge was dragged about and castigated with the lash.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Even high-status professionals like a court physician to the king were not immune to severe corporal punishment (the lash) and public humiliation if charged with a crime, even if the charge was false.

verbatim: He who divulges the mysteries to the uninitiated is guilty of impiety. Now the hierophant certainly does reveal the mysteries to the uninitiated, ergo he is guilty of impiety.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This syllogism relies on the established social and religious rule that specific sacred knowledge (mysteries) must be kept from the uninitiated, and revealing them is a crime of impiety.

verbatim: From Samos he went, it is said, to Lesbos with an introduction to Pherecydes from his uncle Zoïlus. He had three silver flagons made and took them as presents to each of the priests of Egypt. He had brothers, of whom Eunomus was the elder and Tyrrhenus the second; he also had a slave, Zamolxis, who is worshipped, so says Herodotus,iv. 93 sq. by the Getans, as Cronos. He was a pupil, as already stated, of Pherecydes of Syros, after whose death he went to Samos to be the pupil of Hermodamas, Creophylus’s descendant, a man already advanced in years. While still young, so eager was he for knowledge, he left his own country and had himself initiated into all the mysteries and rites not only of Greece but also of foreign countries.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes Pythagoras had to leave his own country (Samos) to travel. In the broader context of the Greek poleis described, the ability to possess land and permanent standing was restricted to citizens, requiring foreigners (metoikoi) to live as guests or under different legal statuses, as seen when Pythagoras settles in Croton.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to vote in the assembly
verbatim: Pythagoras some time afterwards came up withered and looking like a skeleton, then went into the assembly and declared he had been down to Hades, and even read out his experiences to them. They were so affected that they wept and wailed and looked upon him as divine, going so far as to send their wives to him in hopes that they would learn some of his doctrines; and so they were called Pythagorean women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes an 'assembly' (ekklesia) as the central decision-making body of the Italian Greeks. Access to and the power to influence this assembly was the primary political resource of the male citizen group.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in public assemblies
verbatim: Pythagoras some time afterwards came up withered and looking like a skeleton, then went into the assembly and declared he had been down to Hades, and even read out his experiences to them. They were so affected that they wept and wailed and looked upon him as divine, going so far as to send their wives to him in hopes that they would learn some of his doctrines; and so they were called Pythagorean women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The men in the assembly are the ones addressed; the women are 'sent' to him by the men rather than being participants in the political assembly itself, reflecting the standard exclusion of women from such public/political venues.

verbatim: He would avoid laughter and all pandering to tastes such as insulting jests and vulgar tales. He would punish neither slave nor free man in anger. Admonition he used to call setting right.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes Pythagoras's character by stating he would not punish even slaves in anger, implying that the social norm and legal reality allowed for the physical punishment (torture/beating) of slaves as a standard resource of the master/citizen group, whereas free men had higher protections.

verbatim: To honour their elders, on the principle that precedence in time gives a greater title to respect; for as in the world sunrise comes before sunset, so in human life the beginning before the end, and in all organic life birth precedes death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: A general rule of Pythagorean and Greek society was that age dictated the hierarchy of respect and social precedence.

verbatim: I am told by many that you discourse publicly, a thing which Pythagoras deemed unworthy, for certain it is that, when he entrusted his daughter Damo with the custody of his memoirs, he solemnly charged her never to give them to anyone outside his house. And, although she could have sold the writings for a large sum of money, she would not, but reckoned poverty and her father’s solemn injunctions more precious than gold, for all that she was a woman.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights that Damo held the memoirs but was expected to keep them 'inside the house'. The author expresses surprise that she chose poverty over profit 'for all that she was a woman', suggesting the standard expectation was that women did not have independent control over such significant assets or the public sphere.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the men's athletic contest
verbatim: Eratosthenes says, according to what we learn from Favorinus in the eighth book of his Miscellaneous History, that the last-named was the first to box scientifically, in the 48th Olympiad, keeping his hair long and wearing a purple robe; and that when he was excluded with ridicule from the boys’ contest, he went at once to the men’s and won that;
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific instance in the Olympic games where an athlete, despite being of the age for the boys' contest, was permitted (after exclusion from the former) to compete in the men's division. This indicates a rule or convention in Greek athletics allowing exceptionally skilled or physically mature minors to move up to the higher category.

verbatim: Timaeus in the ninth book of his Histories says he was a pupil of Pythagoras, adding that, having been convicted at that time of stealing his discourses, he was, like Plato, excluded from taking part in the discussions of the school
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific disciplinary rule within the Pythagorean sect: members convicted of misappropriating teachings ('stealing his discourses') lose their right to participate in the group's intellectual discussions.

verbatim: But when Empedocles himself made them public property by his poem, they made a law that they should not be imparted to any poet.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following Empedocles' publication of secret doctrines in verse, the Pythagorean society established a formal rule ('made a law') prohibiting the sharing of their teachings with any individual classified as a poet.

verbatim: Moreover, from his abundant means he bestowed dowries upon many of the maidens of the city who had no dowry.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While this describes an act by Empedocles, it reflects the social necessity of a dowry for women in Agrigentum to marry. His intervention provided a resource to a group (poor maidens) that otherwise lacked it.

verbatim: He was generally admired for his excellence in all fields; thus he was generalissimo of his city seven times, while the law excluded all others even from a second year of command.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a law in Tarentum that restricted citizens from holding the office of general for more than one year, illustrating a legal constraint on political/military resources for the general population, which Archytas was uniquely exempted from.

verbatim: There he remained one year and four months with his beard and eyebrows shaved, and there, some say, he wrote his Octaëteris. From there he went to Cyzicus and the Propontis, giving lectures; afterwards he came to the court of Mausolus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of Eudoxus staying with the Egyptian priests and being recommended to them, the text describes him adopting their specific physical requirements (shaved beard and eyebrows). This indicates a religious norm or rule where members of that group or those living among them were denied the resource of retaining natural body hair.

extracted_rules.rule: Citizen right to fight for law
verbatim: Again he would say: There is more need to extinguish insolence than an outbreak of fire, and The people must fight for the law as for citywalls.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is a general maxim attributed to Heraclitus defining the relationship between the body of citizens (the people) and their legal framework, framing the defense of law as a collective right and duty comparable to physical defense of the city.

verbatim: As a proof of his magnanimity Antisthenes in his Successions of Philosophers cites the fact that he renounced his claim to the kingship in favour of his brother.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific hereditary status (kingship) held by Heraclitus's family in Ephesus, which was a resource (political power/office) that could be claimed or legally transferred/renounced within the lineage.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the city
verbatim: He attacks the Ephesians, too, for banishing his friend Hermodorus: he says: The Ephesians would do well to end their lives, every grown man of them, and leave the city to beardless boys, for that they have driven out Hermodorus, the worthiest man among them, saying, We will have none who is worthiest among us; or if there be any such, let him go elsewhere and consort with others.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the practice of banishment (ostracism or similar) where the citizen body ('the Ephesians') has the legal or social power to remove an individual from the community, thereby depriving them of the right to residence.

extracted_rules.rule: Citizen right to hold high military command
verbatim: Melissus, the son of Ithaegenes, was a native of Samos. He was a pupil of Parmenides. Moreover he came into relations with Heraclitus, on which occasion the latter was introduced by him to the Ephesians, who did not know him, as Democritus was to the citizens of Abdera by Hippocrates. He took part also in politics and won the approval of his countrymen, and for this reason he was elected admiral and won more admiration than ever through his own merit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes Melissus, a native of Samos, being elected as an admiral by his countrymen. This indicates that in the political structure of Samos during this period, citizenship and reaching a level of public approval granted the resource of eligibility for high-ranking military office.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as a legislator
verbatim: Parmenides is said to have served his native city as a legislator: so we learn from Speusippus in his book On Philosophers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of Parmenides serving his 'native city' as a legislator implies that citizens of high standing in Elea had the right and responsibility to draft or reform the laws of the polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be manumitted by peers
verbatim: He is believed to have been sold into slavery by [... and to have been set free by] the Pythagoreans Parmeniscus and Orestades: so Favorinus in the first book of his Memorabilia.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While Xenophanes was sold into slavery, the text indicates a social convention or specific act where members of a philosophical school (Pythagoreans) acted to restore the freedom of a fellow intellectual, suggesting a degree of mutual protection among certain groups.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to theological and astronomical education
verbatim: He was a pupil of certain Magians and Chaldaeans. For when King Xerxes was entertained by the father of Democritus he left men in charge, as, in fact, is stated by Herodotus; and from these men, while still a boy, he learned theology and astronomy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific educational arrangement where a young male of high standing (Democritus, son of a host to the King) was granted access to specialized knowledge (theology and astronomy) taught by foreign specialists (Magians and Chaldaeans) left by the Persian King. This indicates that boys of wealthy/connected families had access to private, elite tutoring from foreign experts.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to burial in native city
verbatim: There was a law, says Antisthenes, that no one who had squandered his patrimony should be buried in his native city. Democritus, understanding this, and fearing lest he should be at the mercy of any envious or unscrupulous prosecutors, read aloud to the people his treatise, the Great Diacosmos, the best of all his works; and then he was rewarded with 500 talents; and, more than that, with bronze statues as well; and when he died, he received a public funeral after a lifetime of more than a century.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly cites a law (attributed to Antisthenes) that punished those who wasted their inherited wealth by denying them burial within their home city. Democritus is described as taking specific actions to avoid this penalty after having spent his inheritance on travel.

verbatim: When he was now very old and near his end, his sister was vexed that he seemed likely to die during the festival of Thesmophoria and she would be prevented from paying the fitting worship to the goddess. He bade her be of good cheer and ordered hot loaves to be brought to him every day. By applying these to his nostrils he contrived to outlive the festival; and as soon as the three festival days were passed he let his life go from him without pain
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates that if a family member died during the Thesmophoria, the surviving female relatives would be barred from participating in the religious rites due to the impurity/pollution of death, as evidenced by the sister's concern and Democritus's effort to delay his death.

verbatim: As to the gods, I have no means of knowing either that they exist or that they do not exist. For many are the obstacles that impede knowledge, both the obscurity of the question and the shortness of human life. For this introduction to his book the Athenians expelled him; and they burnt his works in the market-place, after sending round a herald to collect them from all who had copies in their possession.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a legal/social consequence (banishment and book burning) imposed by the Athenian state on Protagoras due to his expressed uncertainty regarding the existence of gods. This indicates a general rule where public expression of religious doubt or agnosticism led to the loss of residency rights.

verbatim: He was the first to exact a fee of a hundred minae and the first to distinguish the tenses of verbs... The story is told that once, when he asked Euathlus his disciple for his fee, the latter replied, But I have not won a case yet.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that Protagoras instituted a system where high-level education in rhetoric and dialectic required the payment of a significant fee (100 minae), effectively limiting access to those with substantial wealth.

verbatim: and that he was so respected by his native city that they made him high priest, and on his account they voted that all philosophers should be exempt from taxation.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a specific legal decree by the city of Elis. While prompted by respect for Pyrrho, the rule applied broadly to the group 'philosophers', granting them a material resource (tax exemption).

verbatim: Athens honoured him with her citizenship, says Diocles, for having slain the Thracian Cotys.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the formal granting of Athenian citizenship to Pyrrho (a foreigner from Elis) as a reward for a specific service. This reflects the legal mechanism where the resource of citizenship is extended to out-groups based on state-recognized merit.

verbatim: Obviously the same thing is regarded by some as just and by others as unjust, or as good by some and bad by others. Persians think it not unnatural for a man to marry his daughter; to Greeks it is unlawful.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author contrasts the legal/normative rules of different ethnic groups. For Persians, the resource (right to specific marital unions) is granted, whereas for Greeks it is restricted.

verbatim: The Massagetae, acording to Eudoxus in the first book of his Voyage round the World, have their wives in common; the Greeks have not.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This identifies a specific social convention belonging to the Massagetae regarding the distribution of marital rights/access compared to the Greek model of private marriage.

verbatim: The Cilicians used to delight in piracy; not so the Greeks.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text frames piracy as a socially accepted or permitted occupation/action for Cilicians, which was restricted or deemed 'unjust' by the Greek out-group.

verbatim: After that he went to Pyrrho at Elis with his wife, and lived there until his children were born; the elder of these he called Xanthus, taught him medicine, and made him his heir.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes Timon designating his eldest son, Xanthus, as his heir. This reflects the standard legal and social norm in Hellenistic and Roman societies where fathers had the authority to determine the transmission of their estate and profession (medicine) to their children.

extracted_rules.rule: Citizen exclusion of non-citizens from markets
verbatim: They say that once, when he saw Arcesilaus passing through the knaves-market, he said, What business have you to come here, where we are all free men?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Timon's question to Arcesilaus implies a social or normative boundary regarding who belongs in specific market areas (the 'knaves-market'). By asserting that the occupants are 'all free men', he suggests a distinction based on status and likely citizenship/local belonging that excludes certain outsiders or those of different philosophical status.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to the gymnasium and physical training
verbatim: At his instigation his three brothers, Neocles, Chaeredemus, and Aristobulus, joined in his studies... furthermore his slave named Mys, as stated by Myronianus in his Historical Parallels.

His gratitude to his parents, his generosity to his brothers, his gentleness to his servants, as evidenced by the terms of his will and by the fact that they were members of the School, the most eminent of them being the aforesaid Mys; and in general, his benevolence to all mankind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly records that Epicurus's slave Mys was a member of his philosophical school and joined in his studies. In the context of the Greek 'School' (which often utilized the gymnasium or shared intellectual spaces), this represents access to education and intellectual community usually reserved for free men.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain one's own earnings
verbatim: Of my slaves I manumit Mys, Nicias, Lycon, and I also give Phaedrium her liberty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The verbatim is an extract from Epicurus's legal will. It demonstrates the rule that a master had the power to grant freedom to specific slaves (both male and female) upon his death, thereby granting them the resource of liberty/self-ownership.

verbatim: And let Amynomachus and Timocrates take care of Epicurus, the son of Metrodorus, and of the son of Polyaenus, so long as they study and live with Hermarchus. Let them likewise provide for the maintenance of Metrodorus’s daughter, so long as she is well-ordered and obedient to Hermarchus; and, when she comes of age, give her in marriage to a husband selected by Hermarchus from among the members of the School; and out of the revenues accruing to me let Amynomachus and Timocrates in consultation with Hermarchus give to them as much as they think proper for their maintenance year by year.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The will outlines a rule of safe-guarding and financial provision for the children of deceased friends (Metrodorus and Polyaenus). It establishes a right to maintenance and dowry from the testator's estate, managed by trustees.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain dowry upon divorce
verbatim: And when the girl comes of age, let Amynomachus and Timocrates pay her dowry, taking from the property as much as circumstances allow, subject to the approval of Hermarchus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the Athenian context described by the will, orphaned daughters of members of the school were entitled to a dowry provided by the estate of the benefactor to ensure their marriageability and social standing.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to own land in Attica
verbatim: They allege... that he was not a genuine Athenian citizen, a charge brought by Timocrates and by Herodotus in a book On the Training of Epicurus as a Cadet.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mentions that enemies of Epicurus attacked his status as a 'genuine Athenian citizen'. This implies that citizenship was a prerequisite for certain rights (like owning the 'Garden' property mentioned elsewhere as being purchased for eighty minae) which were contested if citizenship was in doubt.

verbatim: It is of such a sort that those who are already tolerably, or even perfectly, well acquainted with the details can, by analysis of what they know into such elementary perceptions as these, best prosecute their researches in physical science as a whole; while those, on the other hand, who are not altogether entitled to rank as mature students can in silent fashion and as quick as thought run over the doctrines most important for their peace of mind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epicurus explicitly distinguishes between 'mature students' and those not yet entitled to that rank, providing the latter with a specific resource (the summary/epitome) to ensure they can access the most important doctrines for their mental well-being without needing to master every detail immediately.

verbatim: Many others besides you will find these reasonings useful, and especially those who have but recently made acquaintance with the true story of nature and those who are attached to pursuits which go deeper than any part of ordinary education.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific group of people whose education and pursuits go deeper than the 'ordinary' level, granting them access to more complex reasonings about nature that are not provided to the general public or those with basic education.

verbatim: However, not every bodily constitution nor every nationality would permit a man to become wise.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author presents Epicurean doctrine stating that certain nationalities are fundamentally incapable of achieving the status of a 'wise man,' excluding them from the highest immaterial resource of the school.

verbatim: As regards women he will submit to the restrictions imposed by the law, as Diogenes says in his epitome of Epicurus’ ethical doctrines.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions that women are subject to specific legal restrictions that the wise man must respect, indicating a gender-based differentiation in legal rights and freedom of choice.

verbatim: Nor will he punish his servants; rather he will pity them and make allowance on occasion for those who are of good character.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While the master retains the power to punish, this rule for the 'wise man' grants slaves of good character a conditional resource: protection from punishment based on the master's pity and judgment.

verbatim: And he will take a suit into court.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that the 'wise man' (as a citizen/free person) maintains the right to utilize the judicial system, a standard political and social resource in Epicurean practice.

verbatim: Let no one be slow to seek wisdom when he is young nor weary in the search thereof when he is grown old. For no age is too early or too late for the health of the soul.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epicurus explicitly argues against the age-based exclusion of the young from philosophical study, framing it as a resource that should be available regardless of age.
